layout.$description:
Application frame. The width of the console shell's navigation column
— example code, and therefore the width a consumer copies.

bp.$description:
Breakpoints — SHARED VALUES READ BY JS, not media queries.
Components style themselves with inline style objects, which cannot hold a
media query, so responsive branches are JS (see use-container-width.js).
These live here anyway so the thresholds stay a token, not a magic number
retyped in four components. 768px is what DAMA hardcodes.

dz.$description:
Density (H7 rev.3) — comfortable by default. Data-dense surfaces read these
instead of hard-coded px so a single scope switch re-densifies rows and controls.

text-lg.$description: the largest control text — a data readout inside a chart, where the number is the content rather than a label

text.$description: control text — buttons, inputs, selects, menu items, table cells

text-md.$description: secondary control text — tag chips, pagination, secondary buttons

text-sm.$description: secondary control text — hints, validation errors, badges, legends

text-xs.$description: micro control text — field labels, shortcuts, eyebrow labels

lh.$description: glyph-tight line height — the control reset. Unlike every other dz member this is unitless, so it carries its own $type override: a line height is a number, not a dimension, the same distinction that puts lh in typography.json to begin with. Used wherever a box must be exactly its glyph — an icon inside a button, a standalone icon, an icon-only close/remove control — so the line box never throws the surrounding control out of alignment.

avatar.$description:
Avatar diameters. The first family named after a component rather
than a role, and worth stating rather than glossing: dz and icon are named for
roles. It earns its place because Avatar derives two values from its own
diameter — the initials at 0.4 and the presence dot at 0.28 — and a derivation
needs something to derive from. If a second component ever wants the same
ladder, rename this for the role instead of duplicating it.

logo.$description:
Brand lock-up scale — the mark's slot and the wordmark's size, in
four steps. These are tokens rather than an exemption for one reason: AppLogo
takes the mark and the name from its consumer, so once they are the consumer's
brand these numbers stop being Dravensoft's identity and become the system's
answer to 'how big is a small logo'. A scale is a scale, and Arena's scales are
tokens.

A fixed repertoire rather than one mark-to-text ratio, because no single ratio
is right at every step: a small lock-up needs proportionally larger text to stay
legible, and one constant across the scale would either shrink the small
wordmark below reading size or inflate the large one. The eight numbers are
declared and the proportions that fall out (0.57, 0.60, 0.63, 0.63) are a
consequence rather than a rule.

No number here was invented. sm and md are exactly what the console's Shell and
LoginScreen rendered before they migrated; lg and xl are the approved brand
manual's Primary · horizontal and its large specimen.

mark-sm.$description: beside a product name in an application frame

mark-xl.$description: the hero case — the lock-up is the only thing on the screen
